questions.q:
a. Describe the composition of blood and its importance in the human body.
b. What are the functions of blood in the human body?
c. Explain the process of blood circulation in humans.
questions.answer:
a. Blood is a liquid tissue that constitutes approximately 7% of the total body weight in adults. It is composed of plasma (55%) and various blood cells such as red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets. Plasma is the liquid portion that carries the blood cells and is composed of water, proteins, lipids, and other substances. The importance of blood in the human body lies in its ability to transport oxygen, nutrients, and waste products throughout the body.
b. The functions of blood in the human body are: oxygen transport, nutrient transport, waste product removal, temperature regulation, and blood clotting. Oxygen is carried by RBCs, while nutrients and waste products are transported by the plasma.
c. Blood circulation in humans is a complex process that involves the heart, blood vessels, and blood. The heart pumps blood to the lungs where it picks up oxygen and releases carbon dioxide. The oxygen-rich blood is then pumped to the body tissues, while the carbon dioxide-rich blood is pumped back to the lungs to be exhaled. The process of blood circulation is essential for maintaining life and is crucial for the delivery of oxygen and nutrients to the body tissues.
marking_scheme: Composition and importance of blood (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Explain the process of blood coagulation and its significance in humans.
b. What are the factors that affect blood coagulation?
c. Describe the role of platelets in blood coagulation.
questions.answer:
a. Blood coagulation is the process by which blood changes from a liquid to a solid state, preventing excessive bleeding. It involves the formation of a blood clot, which is a complex process involving various factors such as platelets, clotting factors, and fibrinogen. The process of blood coagulation is initiated when a blood vessel is injured, causing platelets to adhere to the site of injury. The platelets release chemical signals that attract more platelets and activate the clotting factors. The clotting factors then convert fibrinogen into fibrin, which forms a mesh-like structure that traps red blood cells and forms a blood clot.
b. Factors that affect blood coagulation include genetics, age, and certain medical conditions such as haemophilia.
c. Platelets play a crucial role in blood coagulation by providing the initial site of platelet adhesion and activation of clotting factors. They also release chemical signals that attract more platelets and activate the clotting cascade. The significance of blood coagulation lies in its ability to prevent excessive bleeding and maintain hemostasis.
marking_scheme: Factors that affect blood coagulation (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Describe the structure and function of the heart.
b. Explain the process of cardiac cycle in humans.
c. What are the phases of the cardiac cycle?
questions.answer:
a. The heart is a muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body. It is composed of four chambers: the left and right atria and the left and right ventricles. The atria are responsible for receiving blood from the body, while the ventricles pump blood out of the heart. The heart also has valves that prevent backflow of blood. The structure and function of the heart are essential for maintaining blood circulation and delivering oxygen and nutrients to the body tissues.
b. The process of cardiac cycle in humans involves the coordinated contraction and relaxation of the heart muscles to pump blood throughout the body. It begins with the atria contracting to pump blood into the ventricles, followed by the ventricles contracting to pump blood out of the heart. The cardiac cycle is divided into three phases: diastole, isovolumetric contraction, and rapid ejection.
c. The phases of the cardiac cycle are: diastole (relaxation of the heart muscles), isovolumetric contraction (rapid contraction of the heart muscles), and rapid ejection (pumping of blood out of the heart).
marking_scheme: Structure and function of the heart (2 marks)
Phases of the cardiac cycle (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Explain the structure and function of blood vessels.
b. Describe the types of blood vessels and their functions.
c. What are the factors that affect blood pressure?
questions.answer:
a. Blood vessels are the tubes that carry blood throughout the body. They are composed of three layers: the tunica intima, tunica media, and tunica externa. The tunica intima is the innermost layer, while the tunica externa is the outermost layer. The tunica media is the middle layer, which is composed of smooth muscle and elastic fibers. The structure and function of blood vessels are essential for maintaining blood circulation and delivering oxygen and nutrients to the body tissues.
b. The types of blood vessels are: arteries, veins, and capillaries. Arteries carry oxygen-rich blood away from the heart, while veins carry oxygen-poor blood back to the heart. Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels and are responsible for exchanging oxygen and nutrients with the body tissues.
c. Factors that affect blood pressure include the heart rate, stroke volume, blood vessel resistance, and blood volume. An increase in any of these factors can lead to an increase in blood pressure.
marking_scheme: Structure and function of blood vessels (2 marks)
Factors that affect blood pressure (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Explain the concept of blood circulation in humans.
b. Describe the circuit of blood circulation in humans.
c. What are the major components of the blood circulation circuit?
questions.answer:
a. Blood circulation in humans is the process by which blood is pumped throughout the body. It involves the heart, blood vessels, and blood, and is essential for delivering oxygen and nutrients to the body tissues.
b. The circuit of blood circulation in humans begins with the heart pumping oxygen-rich blood to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and releases carbon dioxide. The oxygen-rich blood is then pumped to the body tissues, while the carbon dioxide-rich blood is pumped back to the lungs to be exhaled. The circuit of blood circulation is divided into two main circuits: the pulmonary circuit and the systemic circuit.
c. The major components of the blood circulation circuit are: the heart, lungs, and body tissues. The heart pumps blood to the lungs, where it picks up oxygen and releases carbon dioxide. The oxygen-rich blood is then pumped to the body tissues, where it delivers oxygen and nutrients. The carbon dioxide-rich blood is pumped back to the lungs to be exhaled.
marking_scheme: Major components of the blood circulation circuit (2 marks)
